_comment: Skill Graph rebuild Phase 3 (2026-07-23) — canonical C1 curriculum. Sourced from the standard CEFR progression, sequenced the way mainstream coursebooks order C1 topics — informed by their structure, not copied text. Cross-links back to real a1.json/a2.json/b1.json/b2.json keys where C1 genuinely builds on an earlier foundation. Consumed by POST /api/admin/skill-graph/nodes/import.

nodes.title: Inversion after negative adverbials
nodes.description: Fronting negative adverbials for formal emphasis (Never have I seen, Rarely does one find).
nodes.descriptionForAi: Cross-level: extends B2's basic inversion for emphasis.

nodes.title: Fronting for rhetorical effect
nodes.description: Moving an element to the front of a sentence for stylistic emphasis.

nodes.title: Nominal relative clauses
nodes.description: Using what/whoever/whatever clauses as the subject or object of a sentence.

nodes.title: Advanced participle clauses
nodes.description: Using participle clauses to condense complex ideas in formal writing.

nodes.title: Advanced modal perfect speculation
nodes.description: Layering modal-perfect forms for nuanced degrees of past certainty.

nodes.title: Extended uses of the subjunctive
nodes.description: Using the subjunctive in a wider range of formal fixed expressions.

nodes.title: Native-like ellipsis
nodes.description: Dropping words as fluently as a native speaker without losing clarity.

nodes.title: Complex mixed conditionals
nodes.description: Combining multiple time-frames within one hypothetical statement fluently.

nodes.title: Advanced discourse cohesion and referencing
nodes.description: Using pronouns, ellipsis, and substitution together to hold a long text together.

nodes.title: Advanced connectors (nonetheless, notwithstanding, albeit)
nodes.description: Using rarer, more formal linking words precisely.

nodes.title: Emphatic what-clauses
nodes.description: Using 'What I need is...' structures for strong emphasis.

nodes.title: Shifting register between formal and informal
nodes.description: Deliberately moving between formal and informal register mid-text/conversation.

nodes.title: Advanced academic hedging
nodes.description: Layering multiple hedging devices for precise academic caution.

nodes.title: Passive with reporting structures
nodes.description: Using 'It is said that...'/'He is believed to have...' patterns.

nodes.title: Advanced conditionals with inversion
nodes.description: Formal conditional inversion (Had I known, Were it not for...).

nodes.title: Extending collocational range
nodes.description: Moving beyond common collocations to less predictable, precise ones.

nodes.title: Advanced modification and intensifiers
nodes.description: Using precise intensifiers/downtoners (utterly, somewhat, markedly) for nuance.

nodes.title: Parenthetical remarks
nodes.description: Inserting an aside into a sentence without breaking its flow.

nodes.title: Advanced reported speech nuance
nodes.description: Reporting nuance and implication, not just literal words.

nodes.title: Stylistic variation for different audiences
nodes.description: Deliberately varying grammar/vocabulary complexity to suit a listener/reader.

nodes.title: Advanced quantifying expressions
nodes.description: Precise quantifying phrases (a fraction of, the vast majority, a handful of).

nodes.title: Specialized professional terminology
nodes.description: Field-specific technical terms used in a professional specialty.

nodes.title: Nuanced synonyms for common words
nodes.description: Choosing precisely between near-synonyms with different connotations.

nodes.title: Advanced idiomatic expressions
nodes.description: Rarer idioms used by highly fluent/native speakers.

nodes.title: Academic writing conventions
nodes.description: Vocabulary and phrasing conventions specific to academic prose.

nodes.title: Rhetorical and literary devices
nodes.description: Vocabulary for naming and using metaphor, irony, hyperbole, and other devices.

nodes.title: Philosophy and ethics
nodes.description: Vocabulary for discussing philosophical and ethical concepts.

nodes.title: Economics and finance (advanced)
nodes.description: Vocabulary for macroeconomics, markets, and financial analysis.

nodes.title: Advanced legal terminology
nodes.description: Vocabulary for contracts, legal proceedings, and legal argumentation.

nodes.title: Scientific and technical register
nodes.description: Vocabulary and phrasing typical of scientific/technical writing.

nodes.title: Diplomatic and tactful language
nodes.description: Vocabulary for navigating sensitive situations diplomatically.

nodes.title: Nuanced emotional vocabulary
nodes.description: Precise vocabulary for subtle emotional states beyond basic feelings.

nodes.title: Advanced discourse connectors
nodes.description: A wide, precise range of connectors for complex written argument.

nodes.title: Native-like collocational range
nodes.description: A broad, natural-sounding range of word partnerships across topics.

nodes.title: Subtle register markers
nodes.description: Recognizing small word choices that signal formality/informality.

nodes.title: Advanced cross-cultural communication
nodes.description: Vocabulary and concepts for navigating cross-cultural misunderstanding.

nodes.title: Media literacy and critical analysis
nodes.description: Vocabulary for critically analyzing media sources and messaging.

nodes.title: Corporate and managerial jargon
nodes.description: Recognizing (and using sparingly) common corporate jargon.

nodes.title: Psychological and behavioral nuance
nodes.description: Precise vocabulary for describing motivation and behavior patterns.

nodes.title: Environmental policy terminology
nodes.description: Vocabulary for environmental policy and regulation debates.

nodes.title: Academic research terminology
nodes.description: Vocabulary for describing methodology, findings, and academic argument.

nodes.title: Reading a dense academic text
nodes.description: Understanding a long, conceptually dense academic passage.

nodes.title: Critically evaluating an argument
nodes.description: Judging the strength and validity of a written argument's reasoning.

nodes.title: Understanding implicit authorial stance
nodes.description: Recognizing an author's position even when never stated directly.

nodes.title: Extended literary analysis
nodes.description: Analyzing an extended literary passage's themes, style, and devices.

nodes.title: Reading a legal or contractual text
nodes.description: Understanding key terms and obligations in a legal-style document.

nodes.title: Reading a scientific research abstract
nodes.description: Understanding the structure and content of a research abstract.

nodes.title: Recognizing satire and irony in text
nodes.description: Detecting satirical or ironic intent in a written text.

nodes.title: Synthesizing multiple complex sources
nodes.description: Combining information and viewpoints from several complex texts.

nodes.title: Evaluating the reliability of a source
nodes.description: Judging whether a source is credible and well-supported.

nodes.title: Recognizing an author's stylistic choices
nodes.description: Noticing deliberate stylistic decisions and their effect on the reader.

nodes.title: Understanding a complex academic lecture
nodes.description: Following a longer lecture with dense conceptual content.

nodes.title: Understanding rapid, unscripted speech
nodes.description: Following genuinely spontaneous, fast, unedited speech.

nodes.title: Identifying subtle shifts in an argument
nodes.description: Noticing when a speaker's argument quietly changes direction.

nodes.title: Understanding specialized professional discourse
nodes.description: Following spoken discussion full of field-specific terminology.

nodes.title: Recognizing humor and wordplay
nodes.description: Catching puns, wordplay, and humor in spoken English.

nodes.title: Understanding multiple accents (advanced)
nodes.description: Following a wide range of native and non-native English accents.

nodes.title: Critically evaluating spoken arguments
nodes.description: Judging the strength of a spoken argument in real time.

nodes.title: Understanding nuanced persuasion techniques
nodes.description: Recognizing subtle persuasive techniques in spoken language.

nodes.title: Following a debate with rapid turn-taking
nodes.description: Tracking who says what in a fast-paced multi-speaker debate.

nodes.title: Detailed note-taking for synthesis
nodes.description: Taking notes precise enough to synthesize later into a written summary.

nodes.title: Writing an academic essay with citations
nodes.description: Writing a structured academic essay that references sources appropriately.

nodes.title: Writing a critical analysis
nodes.description: Writing a structured critique evaluating a text or argument's merits.

nodes.title: Writing in a specialized professional register
nodes.description: Writing fluently in the register of a specific professional field.

nodes.title: Writing a literature review extract
nodes.description: Synthesizing several sources into a short literature-review-style passage.

nodes.title: Writing with rhetorical flourish
nodes.description: Using rhetorical devices deliberately to make writing more persuasive/memorable.

nodes.title: Writing a nuanced argument with counterpoints
nodes.description: Anticipating and addressing counterarguments within a persuasive text.

nodes.title: Editing for native-like style
nodes.description: Polishing writing to sound natural and idiomatic, not just grammatically correct.

nodes.title: Writing an advanced formal proposal
nodes.description: Writing a persuasive, detailed proposal for a senior/expert audience.

nodes.title: Using advanced cohesive devices in writing
nodes.description: Weaving referencing and substitution together for a genuinely cohesive text.

nodes.title: Adapting register for different audiences
nodes.description: Rewriting the same content for a formal vs. informal audience.

nodes.title: Presenting a complex argument persuasively
nodes.description: Structuring and delivering a sophisticated persuasive spoken argument.

nodes.title: Engaging in academic discussion
nodes.description: Contributing substantively to a scholarly discussion.

nodes.title: Speaking with native-like fluency markers
nodes.description: Using natural hesitation devices, fillers, and pacing like a native speaker.

nodes.title: Handling challenging questions under pressure
nodes.description: Responding calmly and articulately to hostile or difficult questions.

nodes.title: Using humor and wordplay appropriately
nodes.description: Deploying humor naturally without misjudging the context.

nodes.title: Negotiating at a senior professional level
nodes.description: Conducting a high-stakes professional negotiation fluently.

nodes.title: Delivering a keynote-style talk
nodes.description: Delivering a polished, engaging talk to a large or expert audience.

nodes.title: Discussing abstract philosophical ideas
nodes.description: Reasoning fluently about abstract philosophical questions.

nodes.title: Mediating a complex dispute
nodes.description: Facilitating resolution between two parties in a nuanced disagreement.

nodes.title: Adapting tone for diplomatic situations
nodes.description: Choosing tone and wording carefully in politically/socially sensitive contexts.

nodes.title: Critiquing academic work constructively
nodes.description: Giving substantive, constructive academic criticism aloud.

nodes.title: Speaking extemporaneously with precision
nodes.description: Speaking unprepared while still choosing precise, accurate language.

nodes.title: Native-like rhythm and pacing
nodes.description: Matching the natural rhythm and pacing patterns of fluent English.

nodes.title: Subtle intonation for nuance
nodes.description: Using fine intonation differences to convey subtle shades of meaning.

nodes.title: Advanced assimilation and elision combined
nodes.description: Producing natural connected speech combining multiple reduction processes.

nodes.title: Stress patterns in specialized vocabulary
nodes.description: Applying stress rules correctly to technical/specialized terms.

nodes.title: Minimizing accent for professional contexts
nodes.description: Refining pronunciation for maximum clarity in high-stakes professional settings.

nodes.title: Intonation for persuasive speech
nodes.description: Using intonation strategically to make spoken argument more persuasive.

nodes.title: Matching prosody to register
nodes.description: Adjusting pitch range and pacing to match formal/informal register.

nodes.title: Near-native spontaneous production
nodes.description: Producing extended spontaneous speech with minimal hesitation or self-repair.

nodes.title: Seamless topic shifting
nodes.description: Moving between topics in conversation without awkward transitions.

nodes.title: Self-monitoring for subtle errors
nodes.description: Catching subtle, easily-missed errors in real time while speaking.

nodes.title: Sustaining extended academic discourse
nodes.description: Maintaining fluent, accurate speech over a long academic discussion.

nodes.title: Using filler language naturally
nodes.description: Using natural-sounding fillers (I mean, sort of, you know) appropriately, not as crutches.

nodes.title: Performing confidently under expert scrutiny
nodes.description: Staying composed when questioned by knowledgeable experts.

nodes.title: Challenging authority respectfully
nodes.description: Disagreeing with someone senior or authoritative while staying professional.

nodes.title: Self-evaluating against C1 can-do statements
nodes.description: Reflecting on progress using C1-level concrete can-do statements.

nodes.title: Embracing linguistic risk for precision
nodes.description: Choosing a more precise but riskier structure over a safe, simple one.

nodes.title: Recovering gracefully from public errors
nodes.description: Handling a visible mistake in front of others with composure.
